text.title: Recognition vs. Recall — Principles
text.summary: Core cognitive distinction between recognition and recall, and four design principles that reduce memory load.

section.title: Recognition vs. Recall
section.p: Recognition: user sees options and chooses. Recall: user must remember without a cue. Recognition is always faster, more accurate, and more accessible across user populations.

rule.statement: Prefer recognition-based UI patterns over recall-based ones whenever design permits.
rule.rationale: Recall requires active memory retrieval, which fails under time pressure, cognitive load, infrequent use, and stress — all common in real product contexts. Recognition merely requires matching a visible stimulus to memory, which is far more reliable.

example.note: Menu selection (recognition) vs. command-line with syntax recall. Autocomplete (recognition) vs. blank search field (recall).

rule.statement: Make options visible: show common actions, do not hide them in menus or right-click contexts.
rule.rationale: Hidden options require users to remember they exist and know where to look — double recall burden.

rule.statement: Provide context: keep relevant information visible across multi-step tasks.
rule.rationale: When context disappears between steps, users must remember prior selections — a cross-screen recall burden that causes errors and backtracking.

rule.statement: Use recognition aids: icons paired with text labels, thumbnails for files, previews for documents.
rule.rationale: Icons alone require users to memorize icon meanings. Pairing with text labels converts recall to recognition at minimal space cost.

rule.statement: Support retrieval: implement recent items, search with autocomplete, and frequently-used-item lists.
rule.rationale: Recent items surface what the user has already interacted with — zero recall required. Autocomplete shows candidates matching partial input, reducing the recall burden to a fragment.

section.title: Accessibility Connection
section.p: Recognition-based design disproportionately benefits: users with cognitive disabilities, older users, occasional users, users under stress or time pressure, and mobile users with divided attention. It is not a nice-to-have — it is a baseline accessibility requirement.
